request: We'd like managers to edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed.

problem.value: Managers need to correct or update content in documents that have already completed signature — likely due to errors found post-signing, periodic updates, or business changes.
problem.evidence: "edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed"

actors.value: Managers trigger edit + re-approval. Unclear whether 'manager' = original approver/signatory, a new distinct role, or someone who can override the original circuit.
actors.evidence: "we'd like managers to edit and re-approve"

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval, Signatory are the objects in play per the platform's document/version model.

business_rules.value: Editing a signed document should trigger a new version and re-approval, not an in-place change — but scope of the re-approval (full circuit vs lightweight manager sign-off) and whether it varies by document type/classification is undefined.
business_rules.evidence: context: "edit a signed/approved document" hides supersession

workflow.value: Approval/signature lifecycle and publication/distribution lifecycle are distinct; editing a signed doc likely creates a new version that must go draft→review→signed again, and it's unclear how this interacts with a document that's already published (does the old version get 'superseded' immediately, or only after the new one is signed?).
workflow.evidence: context: two independent lifecycles, supersession pattern

permissions.value: Likely only managers (or a subset scoped by classification/document type) can initiate this; original authors/signatories' rights vs managers' rights not defined.
permissions.evidence: context: permissions forgotten until acceptance testing; classification drives access

constraints.value: Electronic-signature validity and legal hold may restrict or forbid altering a signed document depending on document type/jurisdiction.
constraints.evidence: context: regulatory — electronic-signature validity, legal hold can freeze disposition

reporting.value: An audit trail of who edited, who re-approved, previous vs new content, and prior signatures invalidated/re-collected is mandatory, not optional.
reporting.evidence: context: audit trail is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, should this always create a new version requiring the full original approval circuit again, or can managers apply a lighter re-approval (e.g. their own sign-off only)? Does this differ by document type or classification?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: this decides whether 'edit signed doc' is a full re-run of signatures or a shortcut — completely different build and different compliance exposure.

questions.q: If the document is already published (live) when edited, what happens to it during the new version's review/approval window — does it stay 'current' until the new version is signed, or is it immediately marked superseded/pending?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: conflating the approval lifecycle with the publication lifecycle is the classic trap here — it changes what end users see as 'the current document' during the edit.

questions.q: Is 'manager' here the same person as the original approver/signatory, or a distinct role who can override/re-open someone else's signed document? Do the original signatories need to re-sign?
questions.why: high impact × medium-high uncertainty: determines whether prior signatures must be explicitly invalidated and re-collected, affecting legal validity and UX.

questions.q: Who can trigger this edit-and-reapprove action — all managers on any document, or only managers scoped to certain classifications/document types/clients?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: permissions on signed-document edits are exactly the kind of rule that's forgotten until acceptance testing, and often varies by classification.

questions.q: Are there document types or classifications where re-editing a signed document should be forbidden entirely (e.g. legally filed or under legal hold), versus ones where it's routine?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: electronic-signature validity and legal hold can outright block this feature for some documents — needs to be known before build starts.

questions.q: Is this behavior meant to be standard for all clients, or configurable per client (different re-approval rules, different circuits)?
questions.why: high impact × empty: on a multi-client platform, whether this is hardcoded or configurable changes the entire architecture of the feature.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify the content of a document that has already been signed/approved, and have it re-approved, without breaking the integrity of the original signed record.
summary.scope: Likely involves: creating a new Version when a signed document is edited (never an in-place change), re-running some form of approval/signature circuit on that new version, tracking supersession of the prior signed version, and preserving the prior version and its signatures for audit/legal purposes. Publication status handling during the re-approval window, permission scoping for who can initiate this, and an audit trail of the whole change are all part of the build.
assumptions: Editing a signed document creates a new Version rather than mutating the signed one (per platform's supersession pattern).
'Manager' is a role distinct enough to warrant permission scoping, but its exact rights relative to original signatories are undefined.
The two lifecycles (approval/signature vs publication/distribution) must be handled separately during the re-edit window.
An audit trail (who edited, who re-approved, old vs new content, invalidated signatures) is required given the domain's compliance sensitivity.
Some document types/classifications may need to forbid this action entirely due to legal hold or signature validity rules.
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like a simple 'allow edit + re-approve' toggle, but it actually hides: (1) whether re-approval means re-running the full original circuit or a lightweight manager-only sign-off, (2) what a reader sees as 'the current document' while the new version is mid-approval if the old one is already published, and (3) whether some signed documents (legal hold, certain classifications) must be blocked from this entirely. Without resolving these, the feature risks silently producing documents whose 'current' and 'approved' states disagree — the exact trap this platform's domain notes warn about.

problem.value: Managers need a way to correct or update a signed document's content without losing legal validity or breaking the audit trail — likely triggered by errors found post-signature, renegotiated terms, or periodic updates.
problem.evidence: 'edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed' — the trigger for the edit itself is not stated.

current_process.value: Unknown — presumably today a signed document is either left as-is (a manual side agreement / email) or the whole document is redone from scratch outside the system.
current_process.evidence: No mention of how this is currently handled.

actors.value: Managers trigger the edit and re-approval. Unclear who else is involved: original signatories, a records/compliance officer, other reviewers in the approval circuit.
actors.evidence: "we'd like managers to edit and re-approve"

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval/Signatory — the edit almost certainly produces a new Version rather than mutating the signed one.
business_objects.evidence: Context card: signed content is frozen; edits are new versions.

business_rules.value: Unclear whether any manager can edit any signed document, or only the document's owner/original approver; unclear if this varies by document type, classification, or client.

workflow.value: Likely: signed document → manager edits → new version created (draft) → re-enters approval circuit → re-signed → old version superseded/archived. But whether original signatories must re-sign, whether it's the same or a new circuit, and what state the document is in while re-approval is pending, are all unknown.
workflow.evidence: Inferred from context card's supersession/versioning pattern; not stated by client.

integrations.value: Unclear who/what needs to be notified when a signed doc is reopened for edit (original signatories, readers relying on the old version, downstream systems referencing it).

permissions.value: Presumed: only 'manager' role can initiate edit-after-signature; unclear if this is scoped to documents they own/approved, or global; unclear who can read the document while it's mid-re-approval.
permissions.evidence: "managers to edit" — role named, scope not.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if this re-approval flow should be a standard platform behavior for all clients or configurable per client (e.g. some clients may forbid editing signed docs entirely).
config_vs_custom.evidence: Not addressed; platform is configurable multi-client.

constraints.value: Unknown deadline; likely regulatory implication given e-signature validity concerns.

edge_cases.value: What happens to the old signed version while re-approval is pending? Can it still be read/acted on? What if re-approval is rejected — does the doc revert? What if the original signatory has left?

reporting.value: An audit trail of who edited, what changed, old vs new version, and re-approval history is required — signed documents imply legal/compliance weight.
reporting.evidence: Context card: audit trail is a hard requirement for this product; documents are signed, implying legal defensibility matters.

risks.value: Risk of invalidating prior signatures without proper reconciliation; risk of ambiguity between 'approved' and 'current/published' version; risk of users acting on a stale version during re-approval.
risks.evidence: Context card trap: 'approved ≠ published/current', supersession must reconcile prior references.

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, should this create a new Version (old one retained and marked superseded) or literally overwrite the signed one?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) × high impact: this decides whether prior signatures stay valid/referenceable and whether the audit trail holds — a fundamentally different build from an in-place edit.

questions.q: Does re-approval require the same signatories as the original signature, or can any manager/approver re-sign? And is the original signature invalidated immediately, or only once the new version is itself signed?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) × high impact: determines the state machine, who's notified, and what document is 'live' while re-approval is pending.

questions.q: Can any manager edit any signed document, or only the manager who owns/originally approved it? Does this differ by document type or classification (e.g. confidential/restricted)?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × high impact: permissions are frequently forgotten until acceptance testing, and classification-driven access is a core cross-cutting rule in this platform.

questions.q: While the new version is in review/re-approval, what can other users see — the old signed version, a 'pending changes' marker, or nothing until re-approved?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × medium-high impact: directly affects whether users might act on a stale or soon-superseded version, a named recurring trap for this product.

questions.q: Is there a compliance/legal reason driving this (e.g. a mandated correction process), and does the audit trail need to show old vs new content, who edited, who re-approved, and when?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) × high impact: signed documents imply legal defensibility; an audit trail requirement here is a different build than a simple edit history, and is expensive to retrofit.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify the content of an already-signed document and have it go through re-approval, while preserving legal validity and traceability.
summary.scope: Likely involves: creating a new Version when a manager edits a signed document (old version retained/superseded, not overwritten), re-entering an approval/signature circuit for the new version, invalidating or explicitly re-collecting prior signatures, restricting who can trigger this edit and who can act on the document while re-approval is pending, and logging the whole change (who edited, what changed, who re-approved) in the audit trail.
assumptions: Editing produces a new version rather than mutating the signed one, per the platform's frozen-after-approval rule.
Only users with the 'manager' role can initiate this edit, though the exact scope (own documents vs. any) is unconfirmed.
The workflow re-enters an approval/signature circuit similar to the original, though participants may differ.
An audit trail (old vs new content, editor, approver, timestamps) is required given the signed/legal nature of these documents.
This is likely configurable per client/document type rather than a single universal rule.
summary.blind_spot: The request says 'edit and re-approve' as if it's a simple reopen-and-fix action, but a signed document is frozen by design: the real build is versioning + supersession + signature reconciliation, not an edit form. The client hasn't said what happens to the original signatures, who can still read/act on the old version while re-approval is pending, or whether this right is scoped narrowly (owner) or broadly (any manager) — each of these materially changes both effort and legal defensibility.

problem.value: Managers need to correct or update signed documents (likely due to errors found post-signature or changed terms), rather than start a brand-new document.
problem.evidence: Client states 'edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed' — the need is implied, not the underlying trigger.

current_process.value: Currently, once a document is signed/approved it is frozen per platform standard; there is likely no formal path to reopen it — managers may be requesting a workaround (manual re-upload, external edit) today.
current_process.evidence: Context: 'content immutability after approval' is the platform's default; no as-is process described by client.

actors.value: Managers trigger the edit and re-approval. Unclear whether 'manager' = original signatory/approver, document owner, or a distinct role with override rights.
actors.evidence: Client explicitly names 'managers' as the actor performing edit + re-approval.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval, Signatory — the edit almost certainly produces a new Version rather than mutating the existing one.
business_objects.evidence: Derived from platform's Document/Version model and 'frozen after approval' rule.

business_rules.value: Editing a signed document should trigger supersession (new version, prior one retained and stamped superseded), not an in-place field patch — but this is not confirmed by the client.
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'edit the document almost always means supersede it with a new version, which is a different build from patching a field.' Client's phrasing ('edit... after signed') doesn't specify which.

workflow.value: Unclear whether re-approval restarts only the approval/signature lifecycle, or also resets the publication/distribution lifecycle (e.g., does the previously published version get marked superseded immediately, or only once the new version is re-approved and republished?).
workflow.evidence: Context distinguishes approval lifecycle vs publication lifecycle explicitly; client request doesn't address either.

integrations.value: Who/what needs to be notified when a signed document is reopened for edit and re-approval — prior signatories, downstream systems referencing the old version, readers who acted on it?
integrations.evidence: Not addressed by client; context flags notification routing as a recurring trap on any document-state change.

permissions.value: Only managers (a specific role) can initiate edit/re-approval; unclear if this differs from who may read, download, or approve — and whether all managers or only the document owner's manager.
permissions.evidence: Client names 'managers' generically; context stresses permissions are often forgotten until acceptance testing, especially cross-cutting with classification.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if 're-approval after signature' is a standard capability for all clients/document types, or a client-specific override to the default frozen-after-approval rule.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'frozen-after-approval rule' is standard for all; approval circuits are client-specific — this request touches both.

constraints.value: Electronic-signature validity rules likely require prior signatures to be invalidated or re-collected when content changes post-signature.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'electronic-signature validity' listed as a regulatory sensitivity; 'prior signatures invalidated or re-collected' named as a recurring trap.

edge_cases.value: What happens to the old signed version (retained but stamped superseded?), what if the document was already published, what if re-approval is rejected, what if edit happens concurrently with another read/download?
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; context flags version retention and reconciliation as recurring needs.

reporting.value: An audit trail of who edited what, when, previous vs. new content/value, and the re-approval history is very likely mandatory given the compliance nature of signed documents.
reporting.evidence: Context: 'an audit trail... is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have — retrofitting it late is expensive,' directly triggered by this signed-document scenario.

risks.value: Risk of confusing 'approved' with 'current/published,' risk of stale links/citations to the old signed version, risk of re-approval circuit not matching original signatories.
risks.evidence: Context explicitly calls out these as classic traps for this exact scenario.

questions.q: When a manager 'edits' a signed document, should this always create a new version (old one retained and marked superseded), or is there any case where the signed content itself is meant to change in place?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — this decides between a supersession build (new version, re-signature, reconciliation) and a much riskier in-place mutation of signed content; the whole feature shape depends on this answer.

questions.q: Does re-approval also require re-collecting signatures from all original signatories, or just the manager's own sign-off? And is the re-approval circuit the same as the original approval circuit?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — determines whether this is a lightweight manager override or a full replay of the multi-step approval/signature circuit, which changes UX, notifications and effort significantly.

questions.q: If the document was already published, does editing it immediately mark the published version as superseded, or does it stay 'live' until the new version is re-approved and re-published?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — conflating the approval lifecycle with the publication lifecycle is the platform's #1 named trap; getting this wrong means people act on the wrong version.

questions.q: Is 'manager' here the document's original approver/owner, or any manager role — and should this edit-and-reapprove capability be available for all document types/clients, or configured per client/document category?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — affects both who can trigger this sensitive action and whether it's a global platform change or a client-specific configuration.

questions.q: What must the audit trail capture for this action (old vs. new content, who edited, why, re-approval history), and who needs to be notified when a signed document is reopened (prior signatories, readers, downstream systems)?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — audit trail is a hard compliance requirement here, and retrofitting it after build is expensive; notification routing is a recurring gap.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify a document's content after it has already been signed/approved, and route it through re-approval — while preserving the integrity, signature validity and traceability the platform guarantees for signed documents.
summary.scope: Likely includes: a controlled 'reopen' action limited to authorized managers on a signed/approved document; creation of a new Version (supersession) carrying the edits, rather than mutation of the frozen signed version; invalidation and re-collection of signatures via a re-approval circuit (same or reduced vs. original); explicit handling of the publication state (does the old published version stay live until the new one is approved, or is it superseded immediately); an audit trail capturing who edited what, when, and the full re-approval history; notifications to prior signatories and any downstream consumers of the old version. Likely excludes (pending confirmation): in-place editing of already-signed content, and changes to unrelated document types unless explicitly scoped.
assumptions: Editing a signed document creates a new Version (supersession pattern) rather than patching the existing signed content.
'Manager' refers to a role with authority over the document (owner, original approver, or line manager) distinct from a generic user, though the exact scope is unconfirmed.
The prior signed version is retained and stamped superseded, not deleted, per platform retention rules.
Re-approval will trigger some form of re-signature requirement given electronic-signature validity constraints.
This capability may need to be configurable per client/document type rather than a single global rule.
summary.blind_spot: The request conflates two independent lifecycles the platform explicitly separates — approval/signature and publication/distribution. If the document was already published, the team must decide whether it stays 'live' during the edit-and-reapprove cycle or is immediately marked superseded; getting this wrong means end users act on the wrong (stale or invalid) version, which is the platform's most common and costly failure mode.
